meta.notes: Novel AI-generated PSBB practice questions for the SpeedyCAT proof-of-concept. NOT real MCAT/AAMC content. Text-only. Chunk 2 of 10.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes culture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A group within a society that shares distinctive norms and values while sharing the larger culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The shared beliefs, values, norms, behaviors, and material objects that characterize a group or society.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The idea that deviance results from the labels society attaches to certain acts and people.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Patterns or traits found in all known human cultures.

questions.explanation: Culture is best described as the shared beliefs, values, norms, behaviors, and material objects that characterize a group or society. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of cultural lag?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A community of skateboarders with their own slang, dress, and unspoken etiquette.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A nation celebrating many groups' festivals and languages rather than promoting a single culture.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The same coffee chain and phone brand appearing in cities on every continent.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Laws and etiquette failing to keep pace with a newly invented communication technology.

questions.explanation: An example of cultural lag is: laws and etiquette failing to keep pace with a newly invented communication technology. In general, cultural lag refers to the delay between a change in material culture and the adjustment of nonmaterial culture to it. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which term is best described as follows: the process by which a minority group gradually takes on the culture of the dominant group?

choices.label: A
choices.text: beliefs

choices.label: B
choices.text: a countercultural norm

choices.label: C
choices.text: norms

choices.label: D
choices.text: assimilation

questions.explanation: This description matches assimilation, which is the process by which a minority group gradually takes on the culture of the dominant group. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes a counterculture?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A group whose norms and values actively oppose or reject those of the dominant culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: The specific convictions that members of a culture accept as true.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The rewards and punishments a group uses to enforce conformity to its norms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The process by which societies move toward industrial, urban, and rationalized forms.

questions.explanation: A counterculture is best described as a group whose norms and values actively oppose or reject those of the dominant culture. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of labeling theory?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A teenager acting out more after being repeatedly branded a troublemaker by adults.

choices.label: B
choices.text: Two neighboring groups gradually borrowing each other's foods and holidays.

choices.label: C
choices.text: Someone embracing a criminal self-image and reoffending after being publicly labeled.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Withholding judgment of an unfamiliar ritual until one understands its meaning within that culture.

questions.explanation: An example of labeling theory is: a teenager acting out more after being repeatedly branded a troublemaker by adults. In general, labeling theory refers to the idea that deviance results from the labels society attaches to certain acts and people. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

choices.label: B
choices.text: labeling theory

choices.label: C
choices.text: social change

choices.label: D

questions.explanation: This description matches labeling theory, which is the idea that deviance results from the labels society attaches to certain acts and people. The other options name different concepts that do not fit this description.

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes primary deviance?

choices.label: A
choices.text: The mechanisms a society uses to encourage or compel conformity to its norms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: An initial norm violation that has little effect on a person's self-concept.

choices.label: C
choices.text: The rewards and punishments a group uses to enforce conformity to its norms.

choices.label: D
choices.text: The creation of new cultural elements, whether new objects or new ideas.

questions.explanation: Primary deviance is best described as an initial norm violation that has little effect on a person's self-concept. The other options describe different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of collective behavior?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A nation celebrating many groups' festivals and languages rather than promoting a single culture.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A formerly incarcerated person being treated first and foremost as an ex-convict.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A concert crowd suddenly surging together toward the stage.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Immigrant families adopting the dominant language and customs over generations until differences fade.

questions.explanation: An example of collective behavior is: a concert crowd suddenly surging together toward the stage. In general, collective behavior refers to relatively spontaneous and unstructured action by a large group of people. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following best describes globalization?

choices.text: The increasing interconnection of societies through trade, technology, and culture.

choices.text: The process by which societies move toward industrial, urban, and rationalized forms.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of a countercultural norm?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A formerly incarcerated person being treated first and foremost as an ex-convict.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A group that treats refusing paid work as admirable rather than irresponsible.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A dish from one country becoming a popular menu item in distant nations.

choices.label: D
choices.text: Wearing very casual clothing to a formal dinner and drawing disapproving looks.

questions.explanation: An example of a countercultural norm is: a group that treats refusing paid work as admirable rather than irresponsible. In general, a countercultural norm refers to a behavioral expectation upheld within a group that deliberately inverts a mainstream norm. The other options illustrate different concepts.
questions.sourceLicense: AI-generated proof-of-concept — not real MCAT/AAMC content

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of role conflict?

choices.label: A
choices.text: A system of schools that teaches literacy, job skills, and civic norms.

choices.label: B
choices.text: A clinic redesigned for speed, standardized steps, and measurable throughput like a fast-food counter.

choices.label: C
choices.text: A parent forced to choose between an urgent work deadline and a child's recital.

choices.label: D
choices.text: A manager torn between being a supportive mentor and a strict enforcer of rules.

questions.explanation: An example of role conflict is: a parent forced to choose between an urgent work deadline and a child's recital. In general, role conflict refers to tension that arises from the competing demands of two or more different roles. The other options illustrate different concepts.

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of a formal organization?

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of in-group bias?

questions.stem: Which of the following is the best example of the overjustification effect?
